name: startup-metrics-framework
description: This skill should be used when the user asks about "key startup
capabilities: Calculate MRR and ARR from subscription data
Compute CAC, LTV, and LTV:CAC ratio with benchmarks
Determine burn rate, runway, and burn multiple
Track SaaS-specific metrics including NDR, magic number, and rule of 40
Analyze marketplace metrics like GMV, take rate, and liquidity
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill is a benign educational reference containing markdown documentation about startup business metrics. All static findings are false positives: the backtick patterns detected are markdown code formatting for mathematical formulas (not shell commands), and the cryptographic/network alerts are misread business metric terms (Contraction MRR, NDR). No executable code, no network calls, no security risks.
valueStatement: This skill provides comprehensive formulas, benchmarks, and best practices for tracking key startup metrics including revenue, unit economics, and growth metrics essential for fundraising and operational decisions.
limitations: Does not connect to real data sources or APIs
No automated data collection or dashboard generation
Formulas require manual input of actual values
Benchmarks are general guidelines, not customized advice

useCases.title: Founders preparing for investor meetings
useCases.description: Calculate and present key metrics like ARR growth, LTV:CAC ratio, and runway to investors during fundraising rounds.

useCases.title: Finance teams establishing KPI tracking
useCases.description: Set up proper metric definitions and benchmarks for the finance or operations team to monitor business health.

useCases.title: Advisors providing strategic guidance
useCases.description: Use framework to advise portfolio companies on appropriate metrics for their stage and business model.

promptTemplates.title: Calculate basic SaaS metrics
promptTemplates.prompt: Using the Startup Metrics Framework, help me calculate MRR, ARR, and MoM growth rate for a SaaS company with 500 customers paying $99/month.

promptTemplates.title: Analyze unit economics
promptTemplates.prompt: My startup spends $50,000 monthly on sales and marketing and acquired 40 new customers this month. Average revenue per user is $200. Calculate CAC, LTV (assuming 5% monthly churn and 70% gross margin), and LTV:CAC ratio. How do these compare to benchmarks?

promptTemplates.title: Evaluate runway and burn
promptTemplates.prompt: We have $900,000 in the bank, $120,000 monthly revenue, and $200,000 in monthly expenses. Calculate our monthly burn rate, runway in months, and burn multiple if we added $100,000 in net new ARR last quarter.

promptTemplates.title: Prepare investor metrics dashboard
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me create an investor-ready metrics summary for our Series A pitch. Our data: MRR $250K growing 18% MoM, CAC $1,200, LTV $4,800, NDR 112%, logo retention 92%, burn $180K/month with 18 months runway. Present in dashboard format with benchmark context.

bestPractices: Track 5-7 core metrics intensely rather than 50 metrics loosely - focus on actionable metrics tied to business value
Update core metrics weekly (MRR, growth rates) and full suite monthly for board reporting and investor updates
Always include context with metrics: current value, growth rate or trend, and benchmark comparison for interpretation
antiPatterns: Focusing on vanity metrics like total users or page views without retention or engagement context
Ignoring unit economics at early stages - CAC and LTV matter even at seed stage
Gaming metrics by optimizing for dashboard numbers rather than real business outcomes

faq.question: What is the difference between MRR and ARR?
faq.answer: MRR (Monthly Recurring Revenue) is your monthly subscription revenue. ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) is MRR × 12. Use MRR for monthly tracking and ARR for annual comparisons and investor reporting.

faq.question: What is a healthy LTV:CAC ratio?
faq.answer: LTV:CAC above 3.0 is considered healthy. Above 3.0 means you generate $3 in lifetime value for every $1 spent on acquisition. Ratios between 1.0-3.0 need improvement, and below 1.0 is unsustainable.

faq.question: How much runway should a startup maintain?
faq.answer: Always maintain 12-18 months of runway. This provides buffer for market downturns, fundraising timeline extensions, and operational adjustments without desperate conditions.

faq.question: What metrics matter most at seed stage?
faq.answer: At seed stage, focus on MRR growth rate (15-20% MoM), user retention (Day 7, Day 30), core engagement metrics, and early unit economics baselines. Revenue may still be zero while validating product-market fit.

faq.question: What is the Rule of 40?
faq.answer: The Rule of 40 states that a company's revenue growth rate plus profit margin should exceed 40%. For example, 50% growth plus 10% margin equals 40%. This is a metric for mature companies balancing growth and profitability.

faq.question: How do I calculate burn rate?
faq.answer: Monthly burn rate = Monthly Expenses - Monthly Revenue. Negative burn means you are losing money (typical for early-stage startups). Track net burn (including revenue) and gross burn (expenses only) separately.
